entries.name: counter_leading_intervals
entries.description: For a given counter timeline (e.g
entries.fullDescription: For a given counter timeline (e.g. a single counter track), returns intervals of time where the counter has the same value. For every run of identical values, this macro will return a row for the first one, the last one, and a row merging all other ones. This to to facilitate construction of counters from delta_values. Intervals are computed in a "forward-looking" way. That is, if a counter changes value at some timestamp, it's assumed it *just* reached that value and it should continue to have that value until the next value change. The final value is assumed to hold until the very end of the trace. For example, suppose we have the following data: ``` ts=0, value=10, track_id=1 ts=0, value=10, track_id=2 ts=10, value=10, track_id=1 ts=10, value=20, track_id=2 ts=20, value=30, track_id=1 [end of trace at ts = 40] ``` Then this macro will generate the following intervals: ``` ts=0, dur=10, value=10, track_id=1 ts=10, dur=10, value=10, track_id=1 ts=20, dur=10, value=30, track_id=1 ts=0, dur=10, value=10, track_id=2 ts=10, dur=30, value=20, track_id=2 ```
entries.returnDescription: Table with the schema: id LONG, As passed in ts TIMESTAMP, As passed in dur DURATION, Difference to the timestamp for the leading row. track_id JOINID(track.id), As passed in value DOUBLE, As passed in next_value DOUBLE, Value for the leading row. delta_value DOUBLE Delta to the *lagging* row - note that this is not the same thing as (next_value - value).

params.description: A table/view/subquery corresponding to a "counter-like" table. This table must have the columns "id" and "ts" and "track_id" and "value" corresponding to an id, timestamp, counter track_id and associated counter value.

columns.description: Duration in which the counter is contant and idle state doesn't change.

columns.description: Idle state of the CPU that corresponds to this counter. An idle state of -1 is defined to be active state for the CPU, and the larger the integer, the deeper the idle state of the CPU. NULL if not found or undefined.

entries.name: cpu_process_utilization_in_interval
entries.description: Returns a table with process utilization over a given interval
entries.fullDescription: Returns a table with process utilization over a given interval. Utilization is computed as runtime over the duration of the interval, aggregated by UPID. Utilization can be normalized (divide by number of cpus) or unnormalized. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals.

columns.name: upid
columns.description: Unique process id.

columns.name: process_name
columns.description: The name of the process.

columns.name: awake_dur
columns.description: Total runtime of all processes with this UPID, while 'awake' (CPUs not suspended).

columns.name: awake_utilization
columns.description: Percentage of 'awake_dur' over the 'awake' duration of the interval, normalized by the number of CPUs. Values in [0.0, 100.0]

columns.name: awake_unnormalized_utilization
columns.description: Percentage of 'awake_dur' over the 'awake' duration of the interval, unnormalized. Values in [0.0, 100.0 * <number_of_cpus>]

entries.name: cpu_process_utilization_per_period
entries.description: Returns a table of process utilization per given period
entries.fullDescription: Returns a table of process utilization per given period. Utilization is calculated as sum of average utilization of each CPU in each period, which is defined as a multiply of |interval|. For this reason first and last period might have lower then real utilization.

params.name: interval
params.description: Length of the period on which utilization should be averaged.

columns.name: ts
columns.description: Timestamp of start of a second.

columns.name: utilization
columns.description: Sum of average utilization over period. Note: as the data is normalized, the values will be in the [0, 1] range.

columns.name: unnormalized_utilization
columns.description: Sum of average utilization over all CPUs over period. Note: as the data is unnormalized, the values will be in the [0, cpu_count] range.

entries.name: cpu_process_utilization_per_second
entries.description: Returns a table of process utilization per second
entries.fullDescription: Returns a table of process utilization per second. Utilization is calculated as sum of average utilization of each CPU in each period, which is defined as a multiply of |interval|. For this reason first and last period might have lower then real utilization.

columns.name: unnormalized_utilization

Currently, this table is backed by the following data sources: * Linux perf * Simpleperf proto format * macOS instruments * Chrome CPU profiling * Legacy V8 CPU profiling * Profiling data in Gecko traces

columns.name: id
columns.description: The id of the sample.

columns.description: The timestamp of the sample.

columns.description: The utid of the thread of the sample, if available.

entries.name: cpu_profiling_summary_tree
entries.description: Table summarising the callstacks captured during any CPU profiling which occurred during the trace
entries.fullDescription: Table summarising the callstacks captured during any CPU profiling which occurred during the trace. Specifically, this table returns a tree containing all the callstacks seen during the trace with `self_count` equal to the number of samples with that frame as the leaf and `cumulative_count` equal to the number of samples with the frame anywhere in the tree. The data sources supported are the same as the `cpu_profiling_samples` table.

columns.name: id
columns.description: The id of the callstack; by callstack we mean a unique set of frames up to the root frame.

columns.name: parent_id
columns.description: The id of the parent callstack for this callstack. NULL if this is root.

columns.name: name
columns.description: The function name of the frame for this callstack.

columns.name: mapping_name
columns.description: The name of the mapping containing the frame. This can be a native binary, library, JAR or APK.

columns.name: source_file
columns.description: The name of the file containing the function.

columns.name: line_number
columns.description: The line number in the file the function is located at.

columns.name: self_count
columns.description: The number of samples with this function as the leaf frame.

columns.name: cumulative_count
columns.description: The number of samples with this function appearing anywhere on the callstack.

entries.name: graph_dominator_tree
entries.description: Given a table containing a directed flow-graph and an entry node, computes the "dominator tree" for the graph
entries.fullDescription: Given a table containing a directed flow-graph and an entry node, computes the "dominator tree" for the graph. See [1] for an explanation of what a dominator tree is. [1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dominator_(graph_theory) Example usage on traces containing heap graphs: ``` CREATE PERFETTO VIEW dominator_compatible_heap_graph AS -- Extract the edges from the heap graph which correspond to references -- between objects. SELECT owner_id AS source_node_id, owned_id as dest_node_id FROM heap_graph_reference JOIN heap_graph_object owner on heap_graph_reference.owner_id = owner.id WHERE owned_id IS NOT NULL AND owner.reachable UNION ALL -- Since a Java heap graph is a "forest" structure, we need to add a dummy -- "root" node which connects all the roots of the forest into a single -- connected component. SELECT (SELECT max(id) + 1 FROM heap_graph_object) as source_node_id, id FROM heap_graph_object WHERE root_type IS NOT NULL; SELECT * FROM graph_dominator_tree!( dominator_compatible_heap_graph, (SELECT max(id) + 1 FROM heap_graph_object) ); ```
entries.returnDescription: The returned table has the schema (node_id LONG, dominator_node_id LONG). |node_id| is the id of the node from the input graph and |dominator_node_id| is the id of the node in the input flow-graph which is the "dominator" of |node_id|.

params.name: graph_table
params.description: A table/view/subquery corresponding to a directed flow-graph on which the dominator tree should be computed. This table must have the columns "source_node_id" and "dest_node_id" corresponding to the two nodes on either end of the edges in the graph. Note: the columns must contain uint32 similar to ids in trace processor tables (i.e. the values should be relatively dense and close to zero). The implementation makes assumptions on this for performance reasons and, if this criteria is not, can lead to enormous amounts of memory being allocated. Note: this means that the graph *must* be a single fully connected component with |root_node_id| (see below) being the "entry node" for this component. Specifically, all nodes *must* be reachable by following paths from the root node. Failing to adhere to this property will result in undefined behaviour. If working with a "forest"-like structure, a dummy node should be added which links all the roots of the forest together into a single component; an example of this can be found in the heap graph example query above.

params.name: root_node_id
params.description: The entry node to |graph_table| which will be the root of the dominator tree.

entries.name: graph_next_sibling
entries.description: Computes the next sibling node in a directed graph
entries.fullDescription: Computes the next sibling node in a directed graph. The next node under a parent node is determined by on the |sort_key|, which should be unique for every node under a parent. The order of the next sibling is undefined if the |sort_key| is not unique. Example usage: ``` -- Compute the next sibling: SELECT * FROM graph_next_sibling!( ( SELECT id AS node_id, parent_id AS node_parent_id, ts AS sort_key FROM slice ) ); ```
entries.returnDescription: The returned table has the schema (node_id LONG, next_node_id LONG). |node_id| is the id of the node from the input graph and |next_node_id| is the id of the node which is its next sibling.

params.name: graph_table
params.description: A table/view/subquery corresponding to a directed graph for which to find the next sibling. This table must have the columns "node_id", "node_parent_id" and "sort_key".

entries.name: graph_reachable_bfs
entries.description: Computes the "reachable" set of nodes in a directed graph from a given starting node by performing a breadth-first search on the graph
entries.fullDescription: Computes the "reachable" set of nodes in a directed graph from a given starting node by performing a breadth-first search on the graph. The returned nodes are structured as a tree with parent-child relationships corresponding to the order in which nodes were encountered by the BFS. While this macro can be used directly by end users (hence being public), it is primarily intended as a lower-level building block upon which higher level functions/macros in the standard library can be built. Example usage on traces containing heap graphs: ``` -- Compute the reachable nodes from all heap roots. SELECT * FROM graph_reachable_bfs!( ( SELECT owner_id AS source_node_id, owned_id as dest_node_id FROM heap_graph_reference WHERE owned_id IS NOT NULL ), (SELECT id FROM heap_graph_object WHERE root_type IS NOT NULL) ); ```
entries.returnDescription: The returned table has the schema (node_id LONG, parent_node_id LONG). |node_id| is the id of the node from the input graph and |parent_node_id| is the id of the node which was the first encountered predecessor in a BFS search of the graph.

params.name: graph_table
params.description: A table/view/subquery corresponding to a directed graph on which the reachability search should be performed. This table must have the columns "source_node_id" and "dest_node_id" corresponding to the two nodes on either end of the edges in the graph. Note: the columns must contain uint32 similar to ids in trace processor tables (i.e. the values should be relatively dense and close to zero). The implementation makes assumptions on this for performance reasons and, if this criteria is not, can lead to enormous amounts of memory being allocated.

params.name: start_nodes
params.description: A table/view/subquery corresponding to the list of start nodes for the BFS. This table must have a single column "node_id".

entries.name: graph_reachable_dfs
entries.description: Computes the "reachable" set of nodes in a directed graph from a given set of starting nodes by performing a depth-first search on the graph
entries.fullDescription: Computes the "reachable" set of nodes in a directed graph from a given set of starting nodes by performing a depth-first search on the graph. The returned nodes are structured as a tree with parent-child relationships corresponding to the order in which nodes were encountered by the DFS. While this macro can be used directly by end users (hence being public), it is primarily intended as a lower-level building block upon which higher level functions/macros in the standard library can be built. Example usage on traces containing heap graphs: ``` -- Compute the reachable nodes from the first heap root. SELECT * FROM graph_reachable_dfs!( ( SELECT owner_id AS source_node_id, owned_id as dest_node_id FROM heap_graph_reference WHERE owned_id IS NOT NULL ), (SELECT id FROM heap_graph_object WHERE root_type IS NOT NULL) ); ```
entries.returnDescription: The returned table has the schema (node_id LONG, parent_node_id LONG). |node_id| is the id of the node from the input graph and |parent_node_id| is the id of the node which was the first encountered predecessor in a DFS search of the graph.

params.name: graph_table
params.description: A table/view/subquery corresponding to a directed graph on which the reachability search should be performed. This table must have the columns "source_node_id" and "dest_node_id" corresponding to the two nodes on either end of the edges in the graph. Note: the columns must contain uint32 similar to ids in trace processor tables (i.e. the values should be relatively dense and close to zero). The implementation makes assumptions on this for performance reasons and, if this criteria is not, can lead to enormous amounts of memory being allocated.

params.name: start_nodes
params.description: A table/view/subquery corresponding to the list of start nodes for the BFS. This table must have a single column "node_id".

entries.name: graph_reachable_weight_bounded_dfs
entries.description: Computes the "reachable" set of nodes in a directed graph from a set of starting (root) nodes by performing a depth-first search from each root node on the graph
entries.fullDescription: Computes the "reachable" set of nodes in a directed graph from a set of starting (root) nodes by performing a depth-first search from each root node on the graph. The search is bounded by the sum of edge weights on the path and the root node specifies the max weight (inclusive) allowed before stopping the search. The returned nodes are structured as a tree with parent-child relationships corresponding to the order in which nodes were encountered by the DFS. Each row also has the root node from which where the edge was encountered. While this macro can be used directly by end users (hence being public), it is primarily intended as a lower-level building block upon which higher level functions/macros in the standard library can be built. Example usage on traces with sched info: ``` -- Compute the reachable nodes from a sched wakeup chain INCLUDE PERFETTO MODULE sched.thread_executing_spans; SELECT * FROM graph_reachable_dfs_bounded !( ( SELECT id AS source_node_id, COALESCE(parent_id, id) AS dest_node_id, id - COALESCE(parent_id, id) AS edge_weight FROM _wakeup_chain ), ( SELECT id AS root_node_id, id - COALESCE(prev_id, id) AS root_target_weight FROM _wakeup_chain )); ```
entries.returnDescription: The returned table has the schema (root_node_id, node_id LONG, parent_node_id LONG). |root_node_id| is the id of the starting node under which this edge was encountered. |node_id| is the id of the node from the input graph and |parent_node_id| is the id of the node which was the first encountered predecessor in a DFS search of the graph.

params.name: graph_table
params.description: A table/view/subquery corresponding to a directed graph on which the reachability search should be performed. This table must have the columns "source_node_id" and "dest_node_id" corresponding to the two nodes on either end of the edges in the graph and an "edge_weight" corresponding to the weight of the edge between the node. Note: the columns must contain uint32 similar to ids in trace processor tables (i.e. the values should be relatively dense and close to zero). The implementation makes assumptions on this for performance reasons and, if this criteria is not, can lead to enormous amounts of memory being allocated.

params.name: root_table
params.description: A table/view/subquery corresponding to start nodes to |graph_table| which will be the roots of the reachability trees. This table must have the columns "root_node_id" and "root_target_weight" corresponding to the starting node id and the max weight allowed on the tree. Note: the columns must contain uint32 similar to ids in trace processor tables (i.e. the values should be relatively dense and close to zero). The implementation makes assumptions on this for performance reasons and, if this criteria is not, can lead to enormous amounts of memory being allocated.

params.name: is_target_weight_floor
params.description: Whether the target_weight is a floor weight or ceiling weight. If it's floor, the search stops right after we exceed the target weight, and we include the node that pushed just passed the target. If ceiling, the search stops right before the target weight and the node that would have pushed us passed the target is not included.

entries.name: heap_graph
entries.description: A list of heap graphs (heap dumps) captured during the trace
entries.fullDescription: A list of heap graphs (heap dumps) captured during the trace.

columns.name: id
columns.description: Unique identifier for this heap graph instance.

columns.name: ts
columns.description: Timestamp of the heap dump in nanoseconds.

columns.name: upid
columns.description: Unique PID of the target.

columns.name: dump_reason
columns.description: Reason why the heap graph was dumped (e.g. OOME, periodic, manual).

columns.name: heap_size
columns.description: Total bytes allocated in the heap as reported by the VM.

entries.name: heap_graph_bitmaps
entries.description: Per-Bitmap-object view over the heap graph, with `mId` and `mSourceId` decoded into their pid / storage-type components and (for parcel-received Bitmaps) the sender resolved to a `upid` and process name at the heap dump's timestamp
entries.fullDescription: Per-Bitmap-object view over the heap graph, with `mId` and `mSourceId` decoded into their pid / storage-type components and (for parcel-received Bitmaps) the sender resolved to a `upid` and process name at the heap dump's timestamp. One row per `android.graphics.Bitmap` instance per heap dump. Scalar columns derived from `heap_graph_primitive` (width/height/density/...) are populated only when the trace is an ART HPROF dump (`heap_graph_object_data` populated); on proto-format heap graphs (e.g. `android.java_hprof` data source) they are NULL and the row still appears so callers can fall back to size-only analysis.

columns.name: object_id
columns.description: heap_graph_object.id of the Bitmap instance.

columns.name: upid
columns.description: Process upid that owns the Bitmap.

columns.name: graph_sample_ts
columns.description: Heap dump timestamp. A trace may contain multiple dumps; this column disambiguates them.

columns.name: width
columns.description: Pixel width (mWidth).

columns.name: height
columns.description: Pixel height (mHeight).

columns.name: density
columns.description: Pixel density in dpi (mDensity).

columns.name: bitmap_id
columns.description: Process-monotonic Bitmap instance identifier (mId), encoded as `pid * 10^7 + storage_type * 10^6 + counter%10^6` per frameworks/base/libs/hwui/hwui/Bitmap.cpp. See bitmap_pid / bitmap_storage_type for the decoded components.

columns.name: bitmap_pid
columns.description: Allocating process pid decoded from bitmap_id. Equals the pid of `upid`'s process for valid traces.

columns.name: bitmap_storage_type
columns.description: Pixel-memory storage backing decoded from bitmap_id. One of 'wrapped_pixel_ref', 'heap', 'ashmem', 'hardware'. NULL when bitmap_id is NULL.

columns.name: source_id
columns.description: Cross-process source identifier (mSourceId), canonicalised: the raw sentinel -1 used by Bitmap.java for "no source" is mapped to NULL. When non-NULL, equals the sender's mId at the time of Bitmap.writeToParcel.

columns.name: source_pid
columns.description: Sender process pid decoded from source_id. NULL when source_id is NULL.

columns.name: source_storage_type
columns.description: Sender's pixel storage type decoded from source_id. NULL when source_id is NULL.

columns.name: source_upid
columns.description: Sender's upid at graph_sample_ts. NULL when source_id is NULL, or when the sender pid is not present in the trace's process table at the dump's timestamp (typical for HPROF-only traces with no linux.process_stats data source captured alongside).

columns.name: source_process_name
columns.description: Sender's process name at graph_sample_ts. NULL when source_upid is NULL.

columns.name: self_size
columns.description: Java self_size of the Bitmap object.

columns.name: native_size
columns.description: Native size attributed to the Bitmap. Includes the pixel buffer when it lives on the malloc heap (storage='heap'); near-zero for ashmem and hardware backings since those pixels live outside the Java heap.

columns.name: reachable
columns.description: Whether the Bitmap is reachable from a GC root.

entries.name: heap_graph_class
entries.description: Stores class information within ART heap graphs
entries.fullDescription: Stores class information within ART heap graphs. It represents Java/Kotlin classes that exist in the heap, including their names, inheritance relationships, and loading context.

columns.name: id
columns.description: Unique identifier for this heap graph class.

columns.name: name
columns.description: (potentially obfuscated) name of the class.

columns.name: deobfuscated_name
columns.description: If class name was obfuscated and deobfuscation map for it provided, the deobfuscated name.

columns.name: location
columns.description: the APK / Dex / JAR file the class is contained in.

columns.name: superclass_id
columns.description: The superclass of this class.

columns.name: classloader_id
columns.description: The classloader that loaded this class.

columns.name: kind
columns.description: The kind of class.

entries.name: heap_graph_dominator_tree
entries.description: All reachable heap graph objects, their immediate dominators and summary of their dominated sets
entries.fullDescription: All reachable heap graph objects, their immediate dominators and summary of their dominated sets. The heap graph dominator tree is calculated by stdlib graphs.dominator_tree. Each reachable object is a node in the dominator tree, their immediate dominator is their parent node in the tree, and their dominated set is all their descendants in the tree. All size information come from the heap_graph_object prelude table.

columns.name: id
columns.description: Heap graph object id.

columns.name: idom_id
columns.description: Immediate dominator object id of the object. If the immediate dominator is the "super-root" (i.e. the object is a root or is dominated by multiple roots) then `idom_id` will be NULL.

columns.name: dominated_obj_count
columns.description: Count of all objects dominated by this object, self inclusive.

columns.name: dominated_size_bytes
columns.description: Total self_size of all objects dominated by this object, self inclusive.

columns.name: dominated_native_size_bytes
columns.description: Total native_size of all objects dominated by this object, self inclusive.

columns.name: depth
columns.description: Depth of the object in the dominator tree. Depth of root objects are 1.

entries.name: heap_graph_object
entries.description: The objects on the Dalvik heap
entries.fullDescription: The objects on the Dalvik heap. All rows with the same (upid, graph_sample_ts) are one dump.

columns.name: id
columns.description: Unique identifier for this heap graph object.

entries.name: interval_merge_overlapping
entries.description: Merge intervals when they overlap to generate a minimum covering set of intervals with no overlap
entries.fullDescription: Merge intervals when they overlap to generate a minimum covering set of intervals with no overlap. The intervals are closed (contain both endpoints) and we consider two intervals overlapping (a) the intervals overlap or (b) if the end point of one interval is within epsilon of the start point of the other.

params.name: intervals
params.description: Table or subquery containing interval data.

params.name: epsilon
params.description: Constant expression for a tolerance in testing overlap (usually `0`)

entries.name: interval_merge_overlapping_partitioned
entries.description: Merge overlapping intervals within each partition group to generate a minimum covering set of intervals with no overlap within each partition
entries.fullDescription: Merge overlapping intervals within each partition group to generate a minimum covering set of intervals with no overlap within each partition. For each partition, this macro merges overlapping intervals into non-overlapping intervals. The result contains intervals where at least one input interval is active. For example, with partition 'A': Input: (ts=1, dur=10), (ts=5, dur=12) Output: (ts=1, dur=16)
entries.returnDescription: The returned table has the schema (ts TIMESTAMP, dur DURATION, partitions). |ts| is the start of the merged interval. |dur| is the duration of the merged interval. |partitions| is all of the columns in partition_columns.

params.name: intervals
params.description: Table or subquery containing interval data.

entries.name: interval_self_intersect
entries.description: Given a list of intervals (ts, dur), this macro generates a list of interval end points as well as the intervals that intersect with those points
entries.fullDescription: Given a list of intervals (ts, dur), this macro generates a list of interval end points as well as the intervals that intersect with those points. e.g. input (10, 20), (20, 25) 10 30 A |-------| B|----------| 20 45 would generate the output: ``` ts,dur,group_id,id,interval_ends_at_ts 10,10,1,A,0 20,10,2,A,0 20,10,2,B,0 30,15,3,A,1 30,15,3,B,0 45,0,4,B,1 ``` Runtime is O(n log n + m), where n is the number of intervals and m is the size of the output.

params.name: intervals
params.description: Table or subquery containing interval data.

entries.name: intervals_overlap_count
entries.description: Compute the distribution of the overlap of the given intervals over time
entries.fullDescription: Compute the distribution of the overlap of the given intervals over time. Each interval is a (ts, dur) pair and the overlap represented as a (ts, value) counter, with the value corresponding to the number of intervals that overlap the given timestamp and interval until the next timestamp.
entries.returnDescription: The returned table has the schema (ts TIMESTAMP, value LONG). |ts| is the timestamp when the number of open segments changed. |value| is the number of open segments.

params.name: segments
params.description: Table or subquery containing interval data.

entries.name: intervals_overlap_count_by_group
entries.description: Compute the distribution of the overlap of the given intervals over time from slices in a same group
entries.fullDescription: Compute the distribution of the overlap of the given intervals over time from slices in a same group. Each interval is a (ts, dur, group) triple and the overlap represented as a (ts, value, group) counter, with the value corresponding to the number of intervals that belong to the same group and overlap the given timestamp and interval until the next timestamp.
entries.returnDescription: The returned table has the schema (ts INT64, value UINT32, group_name) where the type of group_name is the same as that in |segments|. |ts| is the timestamp when the number of open segments changed. |value| is the number of open segments. |group_name| is the name of a group used for the overlap calculation.

params.name: segments
params.description: Table or subquery containing interval data.

params.name: group_column
params.description: Column containing group name for grouping.

entries.name: linux_perf_samples_summary_tree
entries.description: Table summarising the callstacks captured during all perf samples in the trace
entries.fullDescription: Table summarising the callstacks captured during all perf samples in the trace. Specifically, this table returns a tree containing all the callstacks seen during the trace with `self_count` equal to the number of samples with that frame as the leaf and `cumulative_count` equal to the number of samples with the frame anywhere in the tree.

columns.name: id

columns.description: The function name of the frame for this callstack.

columns.name: self_count

columns.name: cumulative_count
columns.description: The number of samples with this function appearing anywhere on the callstack.

columns.description: SVE or SME operation with at least one false element in the governing predicate(s)

columns.description: SVE or SME operation with no true element in the governing predicate(s)

columns.name: l2d_access
columns.description: Whether a load operation caused a cache access to at least the level 2 data or unified cache

columns.description: Whether a load operation accessed and missed the level 2 data or unified cache. Not set for accesses that are satisfied from refilling data of a previous miss

columns.description: Wheter a load operation hit a recently fetched line in a cache

columns.name: data_snooped

entries.name: perf_counter_track
entries.description: Tracks containing counter-like events collected from Linux perf
entries.fullDescription: Tracks containing counter-like events collected from Linux perf.
entries.dataCheckSql: SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM __intrinsic_track LIMIT 1) AS has_data

columns.name: id
columns.description: Unique identifier for this thread counter track.

columns.name: name
columns.description: Name of the track.

columns.name: type
columns.description: The type of a track indicates the type of data the track contains. Every track is uniquely identified by the the combination of the type and a set of dimensions: type allow identifying a set of tracks with the same type of data within the whole universe of tracks while dimensions allow distinguishing between different tracks in that set.

columns.name: parent_id
columns.description: The track which is the "parent" of this track. Only non-null for tracks created using Perfetto's track_event API.

columns.name: source_arg_set_id
columns.description: Args for this track which store information about "source" of this track in the trace. For example: whether this track orginated from atrace, Chrome tracepoints etc.

columns.name: machine_id
columns.description: Machine identifier

columns.name: unit
columns.description: The units of the counter. This column is rarely filled.

columns.name: description
columns.description: The description for this track. For debugging purposes only.

columns.name: perf_session_id
columns.description: The id of the perf session this counter was captured on.

columns.name: cpu
columns.description: The CPU the counter is associated with. Can be null if the counter is not associated with any CPU.

columns.name: is_timebase
columns.description: Whether this counter is the sampling timebase for the session.

columns.description: Sampled thread.

columns.name: cpu
columns.description: Core the sampled thread was running on.

columns.name: cpu_mode
columns.description: Execution state (userspace/kernelspace) of the sampled thread.

columns.name: callsite_id
columns.description: If set, unwound callstack of the sampled thread.

columns.name: unwind_error
columns.description: If set, indicates that the unwinding for this sample encountered an error. Such samples still reference the best-effort result via the callsite_id, with a synthetic error frame at the point where unwinding stopped.

columns.name: perf_session_id
columns.description: Distinguishes samples from different profiling streams (i.e. multiple data sources).

entries.dataCheckSql: SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM slice WHERE name GLOB 'cam*_*:* (frame *)' LIMIT 1) AS has_data

columns.name: utid
columns.description: Thread ID (utid) executing this slice.

columns.name: thread_name
columns.description: Name of the thread executing this slice.

columns.name: node
columns.description: Name of the processing node in the Camera graph.

columns.name: port_group
columns.description: Port group name for the node.

columns.name: frame_number
columns.description: Frame number being processed.

columns.name: cam_id
columns.description: Camera ID associated with this slice.

entries.name: process
entries.description: Contains information of processes seen during the trace
entries.fullDescription: Contains information of processes seen during the trace.
entries.dataCheckSql: SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM __intrinsic_process LIMIT 1) AS has_data

columns.name: id
columns.description: The id of the process. Prefer using `upid` instead.

columns.name: upid
columns.description: Unique process id. This is != the OS pid. This is a monotonic number associated to each process. The OS process id (pid) cannot be used as primary key because tids and pids are recycled by most kernels.

columns.name: pid
columns.description: The OS id for this process. Note: this is *not* unique over the lifetime of the trace so cannot be used as a primary key. Use |upid| instead.

columns.name: name
columns.description: The name of the process. Can be populated from many sources (e.g. ftrace, /proc scraping, track event etc).

columns.name: start_ts
columns.description: The start timestamp of this process (if known). Is null in most cases unless a process creation event is enabled (e.g. task_newtask ftrace event on Linux/Android).

columns.name: end_ts
columns.description: The end timestamp of this process (if known). Is null in most cases unless a process destruction event is enabled (e.g. sched_process_free ftrace event on Linux/Android).

columns.name: parent_upid
columns.description: The upid of the process which caused this process to be spawned.

columns.name: uid
columns.description: The Unix user id of the process.

columns.name: android_appid
columns.description: Android appid of this process.

columns.name: android_user_id
columns.description: Android user id of this process.

columns.name: cmdline
columns.description: /proc/cmdline for this process.

columns.name: arg_set_id
columns.description: Extra args for this process.

columns.name: machine_id
columns.description: Machine identifier

entries.name: raw
entries.description: This table is deprecated
entries.fullDescription: This table is deprecated. Use `ftrace_event` instead which contains the same rows; this table is simply a (badly named) alias.
entries.dataCheckSql: SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM __intrinsic_ftrace_event LIMIT 1) AS has_data

columns.name: id
columns.description: Unique identifier for this raw event.

columns.name: ts
columns.description: The timestamp of this event.

columns.name: name
columns.description: The name of the event. For ftrace events, this will be the ftrace event name.

columns.name: cpu
columns.description: The CPU this event was emitted on (meaningful only in single machine traces). For multi-machine, join with the `cpu` table on `ucpu` to get the CPU identifier of each machine.

columns.name: utid
columns.description: The thread this event was emitted on.

columns.name: arg_set_id
columns.description: The set of key/value pairs associated with this event.

columns.name: common_flags
columns.description: Ftrace event flags for this event. Currently only emitted for sched_waking events.

columns.name: ucpu
columns.description: The unique CPU identifier that this event was emitted on.

entries.name: regexp_extract
entries.description: Extracts the first match of a regular expression from a string
entries.fullDescription: Extracts the first match of a regular expression from a string.
entries.returnDescription: The matched substring, or NULL if no match.

params.name: input
params.description: The input string to match against.

entries.name: regexp_replace_simple
entries.description: Replaces all occurrences of a regular expression with a constant replacement string
entries.fullDescription: Replaces all occurrences of a regular expression with a constant replacement string. Note that there is no way to substitute matching groups into the replacement, and all matching is case-sensitive.

params.name: input
params.description: The input string to match against.

params.name: replacement
params.description: The replacement string to substitute in.

entries.name: reverse
entries.description: Reverses a string
entries.fullDescription: Reverses a string.

entries.name: sched
entries.description: Contains scheduling slices with kernel thread scheduling information
entries.fullDescription: Contains scheduling slices with kernel thread scheduling information. These slices are collected when the Linux "ftrace" data source is used with the "sched/switch" and "sched/wakeup*" events enabled. The rows in this view will always have a matching row in the |thread_state| table with |thread_state.state| = 'Running'
entries.dataCheckSql: SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM __intrinsic_sched_slice LIMIT 1) AS has_data

columns.name: id
columns.description: Unique identifier for this scheduling slice.

columns.name: ts
columns.description: The timestamp at the start of the slice.

columns.name: dur
columns.description: The duration of the slice.

columns.name: cpu
columns.description: The CPU that the slice executed on (meaningful only in single machine traces). For multi-machine, join with the `cpu` table on `ucpu` to get the CPU identifier of each machine.

columns.name: utid
columns.description: The thread's unique id in the trace.

columns.name: end_state
columns.description: A string representing the scheduling state of the kernel thread at the end of the slice. The individual characters in the string mean the following: R (runnable), S (awaiting a wakeup), D (in an uninterruptible sleep), T (suspended), t (being traced), X (exiting), P (parked), W (waking), I (idle), N (not contributing to the load average), K (wakeable on fatal signals) and Z (zombie, awaiting cleanup).

columns.name: priority
columns.description: The kernel priority that the thread ran at.

columns.name: ucpu
columns.description: The unique CPU identifier that the slice executed on.

columns.name: ts_end
columns.description: Legacy column, should no longer be used.

entries.name: sched_active_cpu_count
entries.description: The count of active CPUs over time
entries.fullDescription: The count of active CPUs over time.

columns.name: ts
columns.description: Timestamp when the number of active CPU changed.

columns.name: active_cpu_count
columns.description: Number of active CPUs, covering the range from this timestamp to the next row's timestamp.

entries.name: sched_latency_for_running_interval
entries.description: Scheduling latency of running thread states
entries.fullDescription: Scheduling latency of running thread states. For each time the thread was running, returns the duration of the runnable state directly before.

columns.name: thread_state_id
columns.description: Running state of the thread.

columns.name: sched_id
columns.description: Id of a corresponding slice in a `sched` table.

columns.name: utid
columns.description: Thread with running state.

columns.name: runnable_latency_id
columns.description: Runnable state before thread is "running". Duration of this thread state is `latency_dur`. One of `thread_state.id`.

columns.name: latency_dur
columns.description: Scheduling latency of thread state. Duration of thread state with `runnable_latency_id`.

entries.name: sched_percentage_of_time_in_state
entries.description: Summary of time spent by thread in each scheduling state, in percentage ([0, 100] ranges)
entries.fullDescription: Summary of time spent by thread in each scheduling state, in percentage ([0, 100] ranges). Sum of all states might be smaller than 100, as those values are rounded down.

columns.name: utid
columns.description: Utid of the thread.

columns.name: running
columns.description: Percentage of time thread spent in running ('Running') state in [0, 100] range.

columns.name: runnable
columns.description: Percentage of time thread spent in runnable ('R') state in [0, 100] range.

columns.name: runnable_preempted
columns.description: Percentage of time thread spent in preempted runnable ('R+') state in [0, 100] range.

columns.name: sleeping
columns.description: Percentage of time thread spent in sleeping ('S') state in [0, 100] range.

columns.name: uninterruptible_sleep
columns.description: Percentage of time thread spent in uninterruptible sleep ('D') state in [0, 100] range.

columns.name: other
columns.description: Percentage of time thread spent in other ('T', 't', 'X', 'Z', 'x', 'I', 'K', 'W', 'P', 'N') states in [0, 100] range.

entries.name: sched_previous_runnable_on_thread
entries.description: Previous runnable slice on the same thread
entries.fullDescription: Previous runnable slice on the same thread. For each "Running" thread state finds: - previous "Runnable" (or runnable preempted) state. - previous uninterrupted "Runnable" state with a valid waker thread.

columns.name: id
columns.description: Running thread state

columns.name: prev_runnable_id
columns.description: Previous runnable thread state.

columns.name: prev_wakeup_runnable_id
columns.description: Previous runnable thread state with valid waker thread.

entries.name: sched_runnable_thread_count
entries.description: The count of runnable threads over time
entries.fullDescription: The count of runnable threads over time.

columns.name: ts
columns.description: Timestamp when the runnable thread count changed to the current value.

columns.name: runnable_thread_count
columns.description: Number of runnable threads, covering the range from this timestamp to the next row's timestamp.

entries.name: sched_slice
entries.description: Alias of `sched`
entries.fullDescription: Alias of `sched`. Prefer using `sched` instead.

columns.name: id
columns.description: Alias of `sched.id`.

columns.name: ts
columns.description: Alias of `sched.ts`.

columns.name: dur
columns.description: Alias of `sched.dur`.

columns.name: cpu
columns.description: Alias of `sched.cpu`.

entries.name: sched_state_io_to_human_readable_string
entries.description: Translates a single-letter scheduling state and IO wait information to a human-readable string
entries.fullDescription: Translates a single-letter scheduling state and IO wait information to a human-readable string.
entries.returnDescription: A human readable string with information about the scheduling state and IO wait.

params.name: sched_state
params.description: An individual character string representing the scheduling state of the kernel thread at the end of the slice.

params.name: io_wait
params.description: A (posssibly NULL) boolean indicating, if the device was in uninterruptible sleep, if it was an IO sleep.

entries.name: sched_state_to_human_readable_string
entries.description: Translates a single-letter scheduling state to a human-readable string
entries.fullDescription: Translates a single-letter scheduling state to a human-readable string.
entries.returnDescription: Humanly readable string representing the scheduling state of the kernel thread. The individual characters in the string mean the following: R (runnable), S (awaiting a wakeup), D (in an uninterruptible sleep), T (suspended), t (being traced), X (exiting), P (parked), W (waking), I (idle), N (not contributing to the load average), K (wakeable on fatal signals) and Z (zombie, awaiting cleanup).

params.name: short_name
params.description: An individual character string representing the scheduling state of the kernel thread at the end of the slice.

entries.name: sched_time_in_state_and_cpu_for_thread_in_interval
entries.description: Time the thread spent each state and cpu in a given interval
entries.fullDescription: Time the thread spent each state and cpu in a given interval. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals.

columns.name: state
columns.description: Thread state (from the `thread_state` table). Use `sched_state_to_human_readable_string` function to get full name.

columns.name: io_wait
columns.description: A (posssibly NULL) boolean indicating, if the device was in uninterruptible sleep, if it was an IO sleep.

columns.name: cpu
columns.description: Id of the CPU.

columns.name: blocked_function
columns.description: Some states can specify the blocked function. Usually NULL.

columns.name: dur
columns.description: Total time spent with this state, cpu and blocked function.

entries.name: sched_time_in_state_for_cpu_in_interval
entries.description: Time spent by CPU in each scheduling state in a provided interval
entries.fullDescription: Time spent by CPU in each scheduling state in a provided interval. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals.

columns.name: end_state
columns.description: End state. From `sched.end_state`.

columns.name: dur
columns.description: Duration in state.

entries.name: sched_time_in_state_for_thread
entries.description: The time a thread spent in each scheduling state during it's lifetime
entries.fullDescription: The time a thread spent in each scheduling state during it's lifetime.

columns.name: utid
columns.description: Utid of the thread.

columns.name: total_runtime
columns.description: Total runtime of thread.

columns.name: state
columns.description: One of the scheduling states of kernel thread.

columns.name: time_in_state
columns.description: Total time spent in the scheduling state.

columns.name: percentage_in_state
columns.description: Percentage of time thread spent in scheduling state in [0-100] range.

entries.name: sched_time_in_state_for_thread_in_interval
entries.description: Time the thread spent each state in a given interval
entries.fullDescription: Time the thread spent each state in a given interval. This function is only designed to run over a small number of intervals (10-100 at most). It will be *very slow* for large sets of intervals. Specifically for any non-trivial subset of thread slices, prefer using `thread_slice_time_in_state` in the `slices.time_in_state` module for this purpose instead.

columns.name: state
columns.description: The scheduling state (from the `thread_state` table). Use the `sched_state_to_human_readable_string` function in the `sched` package to get full name.

columns.name: io_wait
columns.description: A (posssibly NULL) boolean indicating, if the device was in uninterruptible sleep, if it was an IO sleep.

columns.name: blocked_function
columns.description: If the `state` is uninterruptible sleep, `io_wait` indicates if it was an IO sleep. Will be null if `state` is *not* uninterruptible sleep or if we cannot tell if it was an IO sleep or not. Only available on Android when `sched/sched_blocked_reason` ftrace tracepoint is enabled.

columns.name: dur
columns.description: The duration of time the threads slice spent for each (state, io_wait, blocked_function) tuple.

entries.name: sched_uninterruptible_sleep_thread_count
entries.description: The count of threads in uninterruptible sleep over time
entries.fullDescription: The count of threads in uninterruptible sleep over time.

columns.name: ts
columns.description: Timestamp when the thread count changed to the current value.

columns.name: uninterruptible_sleep_thread_count
columns.description: Number of threads in uninterrutible sleep, covering the range from this timestamp to the next row's timestamp.

entries.name: sched_with_thread_process
entries.description: View of scheduling slices with extended information
entries.fullDescription: View of scheduling slices with extended information. It holds slices with kernel thread scheduling information. These slices are collected when the Linux "ftrace" data source is used with the "sched/switch" and "sched/wakeup*" events enabled. The rows in this table will always have a matching row in the |thread_state| table with |thread_state.state| = 'Running'

columns.name: id
columns.description: Unique identifier for this scheduling slice (Running period).

columns.name: ts
columns.description: The timestamp at the start of the Running period.

columns.name: dur
columns.description: The duration of the Running period.

columns.name: utid
columns.description: Unique identifier of the thread that was running.

columns.name: thread_name
columns.description: Name of the thread that was running.

columns.name: upid
columns.description: Unique identifier of the process that the thread belongs to.

columns.name: process_name
columns.description: Name of the process that the thread belongs to.

columns.name: cpu
columns.description: The CPU that the slice executed on (meaningful only in single machine traces). For multi-machine, join with the `cpu` table on `ucpu` to get the CPU identifier of each machine.

columns.name: end_state
columns.description: A string representing the scheduling state of the kernel thread at the end of the slice. The individual characters in the string mean the following: R (runnable), S (awaiting a wakeup), D (in an uninterruptible sleep), T (suspended), t (being traced), X (exiting), P (parked), W (waking), I (idle), N (not contributing to the load average), K (wakeable on fatal signals) and Z (zombie, awaiting cleanup).

columns.name: priority
columns.description: The kernel priority that the thread ran at.

entries.name: slice
entries.description: Contains slices from userspace which explains what threads were doing during the trace
entries.fullDescription: Contains slices from userspace which explains what threads were doing during the trace.
entries.dataCheckSql: SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM __intrinsic_slice LIMIT 1) AS has_data

columns.name: id
columns.description: The id of the slice.

columns.name: ts
columns.description: The timestamp at the start of the slice in nanoseconds. The actual value depends on the `primary_trace_clock` selected in TraceConfig. This is often the value of a monotonic counter since device boot so is only meaningful in the context of a trace.

columns.name: dur
columns.description: The duration of the slice in nanoseconds.

columns.name: track_id
columns.description: The id of the track this slice is located on.

columns.name: category
columns.description: The "category" of the slice. If this slice originated with track_event, this column contains the category emitted. Otherwise, it is likely to be null (with limited exceptions).

columns.name: name
columns.description: The name of the slice. The name describes what was happening during the slice.

columns.name: depth
columns.description: The depth of the slice in the current stack of slices.

columns.name: parent_id
columns.description: The id of the parent (i.e. immediate ancestor) slice for this slice.

columns.name: arg_set_id
columns.description: The id of the argument set associated with this slice.

columns.name: thread_ts
columns.description: The thread timestamp at the start of the slice. This column will only be populated if thread timestamp collection is enabled with track_event.

columns.name: thread_dur
columns.description: The thread time used by this slice. This column will only be populated if thread timestamp collection is enabled with track_event.

columns.name: thread_instruction_count
columns.description: The value of the CPU instruction counter at the start of the slice. This column will only be populated if thread instruction collection is enabled with track_event.

columns.name: thread_instruction_delta
columns.description: The change in value of the CPU instruction counter between the start and end of the slice. This column will only be populated if thread instruction collection is enabled with track_event.

columns.name: cat
columns.description: Alias of `category`.

columns.name: slice_id
columns.description: Alias of `id`.

entries.name: slice_is_ancestor
entries.description: Given two slice ids, returns whether the first is an ancestor of the second
entries.fullDescription: Given two slice ids, returns whether the first is an ancestor of the second.
entries.returnDescription: Whether `ancestor_id` slice is an ancestor of `descendant_id`.

params.name: ancestor_id
params.description: Id of the potential ancestor slice.

params.name: descendant_id
params.description: Id of the potential descendant slice.

entries.name: slice_self_dur
entries.description: For every slice in the `slice` table, computes the "self-duration": the time spent in the slice but *not* spent in any child slices
entries.fullDescription: For every slice in the `slice` table, computes the "self-duration": the time spent in the slice but *not* spent in any child slices.

columns.name: id
columns.description: The id of the slice.

columns.name: self_dur
columns.description: The self duration for the slice: the time spent in the slice but not any child slices.

entries.name: thread
entries.description: Contains information of threads seen during the trace
entries.fullDescription: Contains information of threads seen during the trace.
entries.dataCheckSql: SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM __intrinsic_thread LIMIT 1) AS has_data

columns.name: id
columns.description: The id of the thread. Prefer using `utid` instead.

columns.name: utid
columns.description: Unique thread id. This is != the OS tid. This is a monotonic number associated to each thread. The OS thread id (tid) cannot be used as primary key because tids and pids are recycled by most kernels.

columns.name: tid
columns.description: The OS id for this thread. Note: this is *not* unique over the lifetime of the trace so cannot be used as a primary key. Use |utid| instead.

columns.name: name
columns.description: The name of the thread. Can be populated from many sources (e.g. ftrace, /proc scraping, track event etc).

columns.name: start_ts
columns.description: The start timestamp of this thread (if known). Is null in most cases unless a thread creation event is enabled (e.g. task_newtask ftrace event on Linux/Android).

columns.name: end_ts
columns.description: The end timestamp of this thread (if known). Is null in most cases unless a thread destruction event is enabled (e.g. sched_process_free ftrace event on Linux/Android).

columns.name: upid
columns.description: The process hosting this thread.

columns.name: is_main_thread
columns.description: Boolean indicating if this thread is the main thread in the process.

columns.name: is_idle
columns.description: Boolean indicating if this thread is a kernel idle thread.

columns.name: machine_id
columns.description: Machine identifier

columns.name: arg_set_id
columns.description: Extra args for this thread.

entries.description: Tracks containing counter-like events associated to a thread
entries.fullDescription: Tracks containing counter-like events associated to a thread.
entries.dataCheckSql: SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM __intrinsic_track LIMIT 1) AS has_data

columns.name: id
columns.description: Unique identifier for this thread counter track.

columns.name: name
columns.description: Name of the track.

columns.name: type
columns.description: The type of a track indicates the type of data the track contains. Every track is uniquely identified by the the combination of the type and a set of dimensions: type allow identifying a set of tracks with the same type of data within the whole universe of tracks while dimensions allow distinguishing between different tracks in that set.

columns.name: parent_id
columns.description: The track which is the "parent" of this track. Only non-null for tracks created using Perfetto's track_event API.

columns.name: source_arg_set_id
columns.description: Args for this track which store information about "source" of this track in the trace. For example: whether this track orginated from atrace, Chrome tracepoints etc.

columns.name: machine_id
columns.description: Machine identifier

columns.name: unit
columns.description: The units of the counter. This column is rarely filled.

columns.name: description
columns.description: The description for this track. For debugging purposes only.

columns.name: utid
columns.description: The utid of the thread that the track is associated with.

columns.name: io_wait
columns.description: If the `state` is uninterruptible sleep, `io_wait` indicates if it was an IO sleep. Will be null if `state` is *not* uninterruptible sleep or if we cannot tell if it was an IO sleep or not. Only available on Android when `sched/sched_blocked_reason` ftrace tracepoint is enabled.

columns.name: blocked_function
columns.description: If in uninterruptible sleep (D), the kernel function on which was blocked. Only available on userdebug Android builds when `sched/sched_blocked_reason` ftrace tracepoint is enabled.

columns.name: dur
columns.description: The duration of time the threads slice spent for each (state, io_wait, blocked_function) tuple.

entries.name: thread_state
entries.description: This table contains the scheduling state of every thread on the system during the trace
entries.fullDescription: This table contains the scheduling state of every thread on the system during the trace. The rows in this table which have |state| = 'Running', will have a corresponding row in the |sched_slice| table.

columns.description: The CPU that the thread executed on (meaningful only in single machine traces). For multi-machine, join with the `cpu` table on `ucpu` to get the CPU identifier of each machine.

columns.name: utid
columns.description: The thread's unique id in the trace.

columns.name: state
columns.description: The scheduling state of the thread. Can be "Running" or any of the states described in |sched_slice.end_state|.

columns.name: io_wait
columns.description: Indicates whether this thread was blocked on IO.

columns.name: blocked_function
columns.description: The function in the kernel this thread was blocked on.

columns.name: waker_utid

columns.name: ucpu
columns.description: The unique CPU identifier that the thread executed on.

entries.name: track
entries.description: Tracks are a fundamental concept in trace processor and represent a "timeline" for events of the same type and with the same context
entries.fullDescription: Tracks are a fundamental concept in trace processor and represent a "timeline" for events of the same type and with the same context. See https://perfetto.dev/docs/analysis/trace-processor#tracks for a more detailed explanation, with examples.
entries.dataCheckSql: SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM __intrinsic_track LIMIT 1) AS has_data

columns.name: id
columns.description: Unique identifier for this track. Identical to |track_id|, prefer using |track_id| instead.

columns.name: name
columns.description: Name of the track; can be null for some types of tracks (e.g. thread tracks).

columns.name: type
columns.description: The type of a track indicates the type of data the track contains. Every track is uniquely identified by the the combination of the type and a set of dimensions: type allow identifying a set of tracks with the same type of data within the whole universe of tracks while dimensions allow distinguishing between different tracks in that set.

columns.name: dimension_arg_set_id
columns.description: The dimensions of the track which uniquely identify the track within a given `type`. Join with the `args` table or use the `EXTRACT_ARG` helper function to expand the args.

columns.name: parent_id
columns.description: The track which is the "parent" of this track. Only non-null for tracks created using Perfetto's track_event API.

columns.name: source_arg_set_id
columns.description: Generic key-value pairs containing extra information about the track. Join with the `args` table or use the `EXTRACT_ARG` helper function to expand the args.

columns.name: machine_id
columns.description: Machine identifier

columns.name: track_group_id
columns.description: An opaque key indicating that this track belongs to a group of tracks which are "conceptually" the same track. Tracks in trace processor don't allow overlapping events to allow for easy analysis (i.e. SQL window functions, SPAN JOIN and other similar operators). However, in visualization settings (e.g. the UI), the distinction doesn't matter and all tracks with the same `track_group_id` should be merged together into a single logical "UI track".

entries.name: tree_structural_partition_by_group
entries.description: Partitions a tree into a forest of trees based on a given grouping key in a structure-preserving way
entries.fullDescription: Partitions a tree into a forest of trees based on a given grouping key in a structure-preserving way. Specifically, for each tree in the output forest, all the nodes in that tree have the same ancestors and descendants as in the original tree *iff* that ancestor/descendent belonged to the same group. Example: Input id | parent_id | group_key ---|-----------|---------- 1 | NULL | 1 2 | 1 | 1 3 | NULL | 2 4 | NULL | 2 5 | 2 | 1 6 | NULL | 3 7 | 4 | 2 8 | 4 | 1 Or as a graph: ``` 1 (1) / 2 (1) / \ 3 (2) 4 (2) / \ 5 (1) 8 (1) / \ 6 (3) 7 (2) ``` Possible output (order of rows is implementation-defined) id | parent_id | group_key ---|-----------|------- 1 | NULL | 1 2 | 1 | 1 3 | NULL | 2 4 | NULL | 2 5 | 2 | 1 6 | NULL | 3 7 | 4 | 2 8 | 2 | 1 Or as a forest: ``` 1 (1) 3 (2) 4 (2) 6 (3) | | 2 (1) 7 (2) / \ 5 (1) 8 (1) ```
entries.returnDescription: The returned table has the schema (id LONG, parent_id LONG, group_key LONG).

params.name: tree_table
params.description: A table/view/subquery corresponding to a tree which should be partitioned. This table must have the columns "id", "parent_id" and "group_key". Note: the columns must contain uint32 similar to ids in trace processor tables (i.e. the values should be relatively dense and close to zero). The implementation makes assumptions on this for performance reasons and, if this criteria is not, can lead to enormous amounts of memory being allocated.
